skill.name: diasporic-intelligence
skill.description: Use when writing, reviewing, citing, or implementing Diasporic Intelligence, a source-credited framework also described as Afrotemporalism in Action. Preserves source credit, consent governance, provenance, revocation, lineage boundaries, and the distinction from generic cultural AI, biometric extraction, and ancestor impersonation.
skill.summary: A consent-governed framework for citing and applying Diasporic Intelligence with source credit, provenance, and revocation intact.
skill.author: MinistaJazz

security_audit.summary: This skill consists entirely of markdown documentation and a small YAML interface file describing the Diasporic Intelligence framework. The 71 static findings are all false positives: the 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts mismatch on common documentation words, the 'external_commands' findings are markdown code-fence backticks showing an npx install command (not actual execution), the 'network' finding is a public CC BY 4.0 license URL, and 'reconnaissance' alerts mismatch on benign phrases in prose. No executable code, no network calls, and no shell execution exist in this skill.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Mistaken For Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Backticks in README.md fences a documentation example showing 'npx skills add MinistaJazz/diasporic-intelligence'. This is illustrative install documentation in a fenced bash code block, not actual command execution. The repository contains only markdown and a YAML config; nothing in the skill executes this command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Verified by reading README.md lines 89-91; the backticks delimit a documentation code block, not an executable handler.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Public License Reference
low_findings.description: LICENSE.md line 23 contains the URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ as the canonical reference to the CC BY 4.0 license text. This is a static documentation link and does not initiate any network request.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed by reading LICENSE.md; the URL is a license reference in a CC BY 4.0 statement, a universally legitimate citation.

content.user_title: Cite And Apply Diasporic Intelligence With Source Credit
content.value_statement: Teams using cultural AI terminology often strip source credit, consent, and revocation requirements, which flattens the framework into generic diversity language. This skill keeps Diasporic Intelligence attached to its author, governance requirements, and lineage boundaries when writing, reviewing, or implementing AI systems.
actual_capabilities: Provides the canonical, short, technical, and plain-language definitions of Diasporic Intelligence.
Lists required attribution language and citation formats for grants, dissertations, and product copy.
Names safe and unsafe terminology, including the boundary against ancestor impersonation.
Defines the Core DI Minimum: consent states, provenance, revocation, role boundaries, and documented limitations.
Offers a Boundary Test with calibration examples for aligned, borderline, and refused uses.
Distinguishes Diasporic Intelligence from Afrofuturism, voice cloning, generic cultural AI, and DEI initiatives.
limitations: This is a definitional and attribution skill, not a runtime detector or repair tool for live agent behavior.
It is a normative document and not a legal enforcement mechanism for the framework.
It does not grant access to private implementations such as the DIG Method, ZORAs, or Voice Bible.
It does not provide clinical, legal, or pastoral authority and is not a substitute for human care.

use_cases.title: Reviewing Grant Or Dissertation Language
use_cases.description: Apply the Boundary Test and required attribution to grant proposals or academic writing that uses the term Diasporic Intelligence.

use_cases.title: Auditing Product Copy For Cultural AI Claims
use_cases.description: Check marketing or product documentation for ancestor impersonation, missing consent, or extraction patterns before publication.

use_cases.title: Designing Consent-Governed AI Architecture
use_cases.description: Use the Core DI Minimum and Technical Floor to design consent states, provenance metadata, audit logs, and revocation paths.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Explain The Term
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the diasporic-intelligence skill, give the short canonical definition of Diasporic Intelligence and cite the source.
prompt_templates.scenario: Introductory writing or social media post

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: Review A Paragraph
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this paragraph using the diasporic-intelligence skill. Check for source credit, consent, provenance, revocation, and ancestor impersonation. Suggest revisions.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Audit A Product Claim
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the Boundary Test to this product description. Mark each statement as aligned, borderline, or refused, and explain which Core DI Minimum requirement is missing.

prompt_templates.title: Expert: Architect Consent Governance
prompt_templates.prompt: Propose a consent state machine, provenance metadata schema, and revocation API for a dialogic AI that intends to implement Diasporic Intelligence. Reference the Technical Floor requirements.

output_examples.input: Explain Diasporic Intelligence in one sentence.
output_examples.output: Diasporic Intelligence, as defined by Rev. Jasmaine Acelia Cook-Kendrick (Minista Jazz), is the structured, consent-governed transmission of cultural, spiritual, and lineage wisdom across generations through registered voice, dialogic AI, and community accountability.

output_examples.input: Is marketing a cloned elder voice as an AI ancestor aligned with this framework?
output_examples.output: No. This usage is refused under the Boundary Test. It involves ancestor impersonation, likely lacks documented consent and provenance, and uses the term for cultural credibility without the governance requirements.

output_examples.input: List the minimum technical conditions for a system to qualify.
output: Explicit consent states tied to each source record.
Traceable provenance from each output back to source records.
Revocation and correction paths that affect future system behavior.
Non-impersonation role boundaries enforced by the system.
Documented limitations in plain language for the source or steward.

best_practices: Always include attribution to Rev. Jasmaine Acelia Cook-Kendrick (Minista Jazz) close to the first use of the term.
Pair the definition with consent, provenance, and revocation language so the governance frame travels with the name.
Apply the Boundary Test before publishing any product copy or technical claim that uses the term.
anti_patterns: Using Diasporic Intelligence as a synonym for diverse AI, multicultural AI, or a DEI initiative.
Branding chatbots or voice clones as AI ancestors, synthetic ancestors, or spiritual authorities.
Citing the term while stripping consent, provenance, revocation, or community accountability requirements.

faq.question: Is this skill a runtime tool or a documentation framework?
faq.answer: It is a definitional and attribution skill. It governs how the term is used, cited, and bounded. It does not detect or repair live agent behavior during a conversation.

faq.question: Can I use the term commercially?
faq.answer: Yes, under CC BY 4.0 with attribution. However, commercial use that omits consent, provenance, revocation, or community accountability is not Diasporic Intelligence as defined in this framework.

faq.question: How is this different from Afrofuturism?
faq.answer: Afrofuturism is an artistic and philosophical tradition. Diasporic Intelligence is a consent-governed transmission and AI-support framework that operationalizes lineage wisdom through registered voice and dialogic AI.

faq.question: Does this skill let me access the DIG Method or Voice Bible?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides the open practice layer only. It does not grant rights to private implementations, source code, registrant data, or governance records.

faq.question: What if my system cannot honor revocation?
faq.answer: Then the system does not meet the Core DI Minimum and should not present itself as an implementation of Diasporic Intelligence. It may be inspired by the concept but should be described that way.

faq.question: Who is named as the source of this operational definition?
faq.answer: Rev. Jasmaine Acelia Cook-Kendrick, also known as Minista Jazz, through Much Different World. Attribution close to first use is part of correct use within this framework.
